title: A protocol for wide-scope non-target analysis of contaminants in small amounts of biota using bead beating tissuelyser extraction and LC-HRMS
author: Gil-Solsona, Rodriguez-Mozaz, et al.

steps.title: Analytical protocol
steps.instruction: The extraction protocol was adapted from Santos etô al. [1]. For both krill and fish, 0.1ô g of dried matrix was used.1.Weight 1ô g of zirconium beads and add them to the extraction tube.2.Homogenize the samples in a ceramic mortar (previously cleaned with Milli-Q water, ethanol and dried).3.Weight 0.1ô g of each sample and add them into the extraction tube.4.Add surrogate standards solution (see Note #1). Allow evaporation of solvent 30ã60ô min.5.Add 1ô mL of the extraction mixture composed by citrate buffer and acetonitrile (see Note #2).6.Carry out the sample extraction using tissuelyser (total time: 30ô s, power: 5.5). Cryolys can be used instead of Precellys to avoid sample heating during the extraction.7.Centrifuge the samples (11.000 rcf for 10ô min).8.Transfer the supernatant into a glass tube.9.Repeat steps 4ã7 two times and collect the supernatant always in the same tube.10.Reduce the volume of the extracts (to 50%, approx. 1.5ô mL) using a N2 evaporator in order to eliminate the excess of organic solvent.11.Place the extract in a glass bottle with 100ô mL of HPLC-grade water at pH 6.5 adjusted with ammonia and formic acid.12.Clean the glass tubes with 3ô mL of HPLC-grade water (three times).13.Stabilize the mixed-mode SPE cartridges (see Note #3) with HPLC-grade methanol and HPLC-grade water at gravity.14.Load the cartridges with the sample volume at 1drop sã1 approx.15.Dry the cartridges passing air through it for 3ô min.16.Elute the cartridges with 4ô mL of Mixture A (see Note #4). Then, circulate air for 2ô min.17.Elute the cartridges with 2ô mL of Mixture B (see Note #5). Then, circulate air for 2ô min.18.Reduce the sample extracts (in glass tube) to the minimum volume with a gentle steam of N2.19.Transfer the extracts from the glass tubes to HPLC vials.20.Add 200 ôçL of methanol to the glass tubes and vortex for 15ô s.21.Transfer the methanol-extracts from glass tubes to the HPLC vials.22.Repeat steps 19ã20 three times.23.Bring sample extracts (in HPLC vial) to dryness with a gentle N2 stream.24.Reconstitute the residue with 0.5ô mL of HPLC-grade methanol.25.Add 0.5ô mL of HPLC-grade water.

steps.title: Analytical protocol
steps.instruction: Note #2: In order to prepare the solvents for the extractions, two individual solutions were prepared. For the first one (S1), weigh 19.213ô g of citric acid and dissolve them in 1000ô mL of HPLC water. For the second one (S2), weigh 14.705ô g of tri-sodium citrate 2-hydrate and dissolve them in 1000ô mL of HPLC water. Mix 118ô mL of S1 with 82ô mL of S2 and mix carefully. Then, add 200ô mL volume of S1:S2 (59:41) mixture to 200ô mL acetonitrile.

steps.title: Analytical protocol
steps.instruction: Note #3: The homemade cartridges used were those previously described in the literature (Gago-Ferrero etô al. [2]). Cartridges contains 0.2ô g of Sepra ZT,0.1ô g Sepra ZTL-WCX, 0.1ô g Sepra ZTL-WAX and 0.15ô g of Isolute ENV+ from Biotage.

substeps.instruction: Note #3: The homemade cartridges used were those previously described in the literature (Gago-Ferrero etô al. [2]). Cartridges contains
g of Isolute ENV+ from Biotage.

steps.title: Analytical protocol
steps.instruction: Note #4: Mixture A has been prepared by mixing methanol, (470ô mL), ethyl acetate (470ô mL) and 32% ammonia solution (60ô mL). Final concentration of ammonia is 2%.

steps.title: Analytical protocol
steps.instruction: Note #5: Mixture B has been prepared by mixing Methanol (490ô mL), Ethyl Acetate (490ô mL) and formic acid 88ã90% (20ô mL). Final concentration of formic acid is 1.8%.

steps.title: Instrumental analysis
steps.instruction: After sample extraction, 10 ôçL of sample extracts are directly injected (avoiding filtration steps in order to minimize compound losses as much as possible) in the UPLC-HRMS instrument under the following chromatographic conditions:LC parameters positive ionization modeColumnAcquity UPLC C18 column (100ô û, 1.8ô ôçm, 2.1ô ûô 100ô mm)Column temperature40ô ô¯CMobile phase A0.1% formic acid in methanolMobile phase B0.1% formic acid in waterSample volume10 ôçLGradient(%A): Initial 5%, 75% at 7ô min, 100% at 10ô min, 100% at 15ô min, 5% at 17ô min and 5% at 23ô min.LC parameters negative ionization modeColumnAcquity UPLC C18 column (100ô û, 1.8ô ôçm, 2.1ô ûô 100ô mm)Column temperature40ô ô¯CMobile phase A5ô mM ammonium acetate in methanolMobile phase B5ô mM ammonium acetate in waterSample volume10 ôçLGradient(%A): Initial 5%, 50% at 3ô min, 90% at 6ô min, 100% at 13ô min, 100% at 17ô min, 5% at 18ô min, and 5% at 20ô min.

steps.title: Instrumental analysis
steps.instruction: For HRMS, parameters applied for the analysis were:HRMS parameters for positive (+) and negative (-) ionization modesSpray voltage3000 (+), 2800 (-)Capillary temperature350ô ô¯C (+) & (-)Sheath gas40 (+) & (-)Aux Gas10 (+) & (-)Max. Spray current100 (+) & (-)Probe heater temp.350 (+) & (-)S-Lens RF Level60 (+) & (-)

steps.title: Instrumental analysis
steps.instruction: Samples were analysed in a Q-Exactive Orbitrap mass analyser (Thermo Scientific) in Data Dependent acquisition (DDA), acquiring MS/MS for the 5 most intense ions and Data Independent (DIA) analysis at 35ô eV of collision energy in both cases for MS/MS and high energy functions.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: The method performance was evaluated with a set of compounds including pharmaceuticals, personal care products, herbicides, food additives and other industrial chemicals. The selected compounds comprised a wide range of polarity (logP comprised between ã1.16 and 7.03) to ensure a good method performance in both the extraction and the clean-up steps in each sample (see Tableô 1).

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: The linear dynamic range, based on linear regression calibration curves prepared in each matrix, was studied in standard solution at four different concentration levels, ranging from 25 to 500ô ôçg Lã1. All areas were integrated using the extracted ion chromatogram for the corresponding m/z of the parent ion with a window of 5ô ppm. A good linearity range was obtained for almost all compounds. Out of the evaluated 27 compounds, 18 and 20 showed coefficient R2 > 0.98 in krill and lantern fish, respectively.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: In order to evaluate the extraction efficiency absolute recoveries were determined by spiking both krill and lantern fish with a standard mixture at two concentrations (20 and 150ô ng gã1). Recovery values between 50 and 130% were obtained for >70% of compounds. Few compounds showed values out of this range indicating poor extraction and/or important matrix effects. Overall results were very satisfactory considering the wide range of physicochemical properties covered by the selected compounds and the complexity of the biologic matrices of interest. The method precision was estimated with repeatability values, in terms of %RSD, for three experimental replicates, showing values below 20% in most cases.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction:
Limits of detection were estimated based on the signal to noise (S/N) ratios of low concentration matrix calibration standards. The method limits of detection (equivalent of S/Nô =ô 3) obtained varied from 0.01 to 10.67ô ng gã1
d.w. and from 0.01 to 15.79ô ng gã1
d.w. for krill and lantern fish, respectively. Results show the overall good sensitivity of the methodology for the screening of a wide range of xenobiotics in complex biota samples.

substeps.instruction: Limits of detection were estimated based on the signal to noise (S/N) ratios of low concentration matrix calibration standards. The method limits of detection (equivalent of S/Nô =ô 3) obtained varied from

ng gã1
d.w. for krill and lantern fish, respectively. Results show the overall good sensitivity of the methodology for the screening of a wide range of xenobiotics in complex biota samples.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Matrix effects (signal suppression or enhancement) were also calculated for the selected compounds in both matrices according to Eq.ô (1):(1)Matrixeffect(%):((AreamatrixãAreablank)Areasolventã1)x100

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Where Area matrix corresponds to the response given by the instrument for the selected compound in a spiked matrix sample, Area blank is the response given by the instrument in a non-spiked matrix samples and Area solvent is the response given by the instrument in a solvent spiked sample.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Almost all compounds showed ion suppression, with values ranging from ã4% to ã92% in krill and from ã14% to ã82% in lantern fish extracts. Only hydroxychloroquine and pilocarpine showed ion enhancement in both matrices.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Overall, the developed methodology showed a very good performance for the determination of a wide range of xenobiotics in biological matrices. This is particularly certain considering the differences in polarity and other physicochemical properties of the selected compounds for the validation step. However, if the final aim of the user is to provide reliable quantitative data of new compounds identified through suspect and non-target approaches, it is advisable to fully validate the methodology for the newly identified substances.
